questions.stem: In a DBMS interview, you are asked to name the four ACID properties of a transaction. Which answer is complete and correct?
options: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
Availability, Consistency, Integrity, Durability
Atomicity, Caching, Isolation, Distribution
Accuracy, Consistency, Idempotency, Durability
questions.explanation: ACID is Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the classic transactional guarantees.

questions.stem: An interviewer asks: what does Atomicity guarantee if a multi-statement transfer fails halfway? Which response is most accurate?
options: Partial updates may remain until a nightly cleanup job
All statements succeed together or none take effect
Only the last statement is rolled back automatically
Readers always see intermediate balances during the failure
questions.explanation: Atomicity means the transaction is all-or-nothing; a mid-flight failure rolls everything back.

questions.stem: You are asked: Consistency in ACID vs Consistency in CAP — how do they differ at a high level? Which is best?
options: They are identical synonyms used interchangeably in papers
ACID Consistency means replicas eventually agree on values
ACID: integrity rules hold; CAP Consistency: every read sees latest write
CAP Consistency only applies to single-node relational databases
questions.explanation: ACID Consistency is about invariants/constraints; CAP Consistency is linearizability across replicas.

questions.stem: CAP theorem at interview depth: after a network partition, what must a distributed system choose between?
options: Throughput versus storage cost exclusively
B-tree indexes versus hash indexes exclusively
SQL versus NoSQL query languages only
Strong consistency versus availability for that partition window
questions.explanation: During a partition, you trade Consistency vs Availability while Partition tolerance is assumed.

questions.stem: What does 1NF primarily require of a relational table design? Pick the most accurate interview answer.
options: Atomic attribute values — no repeating groups or nested tables in a cell
Every non-key attribute must depend on the whole composite key
No transitive dependencies from non-key attributes to other non-keys
Every determinant of a candidate key must itself be a candidate key
questions.explanation: 1NF: domains are atomic; cells hold single values, not lists or nested relations.

questions.stem: A table has composite key (order_id, product_id) and column warehouse_city depending only on product_id. Which normal form is violated?
options: First normal form only, because city is a string
Second normal form — partial dependency on part of the key
Boyce–Codd normal form, but never second normal form
Third normal form only; 2NF is already satisfied here
questions.explanation: Partial dependency of a non-key attribute on part of a composite key breaks 2NF.

questions.stem: Interview prompt: define a primary key versus a candidate key. Which statement is most precise?
options: Primary key is any indexed column; candidate keys are foreign keys
Candidate keys may contain nulls; primary keys must be composite
Candidate keys uniquely identify rows; one chosen candidate is the primary key
Primary keys are only surrogate integers; candidate keys cannot be natural
questions.explanation: All candidate keys uniquely identify rows; the designer designates one as the primary key.

questions.stem: What is a foreign key's main role in a relational schema? Choose the best interview-ready answer.
options: It speeds up every SELECT by replacing the need for indexes
It stores a duplicate copy of the entire parent row
It guarantees that joins always run in O(1) time
It enforces referential integrity between related tables' rows
questions.explanation: Foreign keys reference a parent key and enforce referential integrity on insert/update/delete.

questions.stem: B-tree versus hash index — when do interviewers expect you to prefer a B-tree? Which answer fits?
options: Range predicates, ORDER BY, and inequality filters on the key
Only for equality lookups on unordered GUID primary keys
Only when the table has fewer than ten rows total
Never; hash indexes dominate all relational workloads
questions.explanation: B-trees support ordered range scans and sorting; hash indexes shine mainly on equality.

questions.stem: When is a hash index typically a better fit than a B-tree in DBMS discussions?
options: For BETWEEN queries spanning large key ranges
For exact equality lookups when ranges are not needed
For prefix LIKE 'abc%' pattern matching on text
For sorting results without an explicit ORDER BY clause
questions.explanation: Hash indexes are strong for = lookups; they do not support ordered range access.

questions.stem: Isolation in ACID: what problem does it primarily aim to control between concurrent transactions?
options: Disk failures that corrupt the write-ahead log permanently
Schema drift when columns are renamed across releases
Interference where one transaction's partial work affects another
Network latency between the client and the database proxy
questions.explanation: Isolation limits how concurrent transactions see each other's uncommitted or intermediate state.

questions.stem: What is a dirty read in concurrency control terminology? Select the most accurate definition.
options: Reading a row that another transaction has committed and then deleted
Reading past the end of a table scan due to a missing index
Reading the same committed row twice and seeing identical values
Reading uncommitted data written by another still-running transaction
questions.explanation: A dirty read sees another transaction's uncommitted writes that may later roll back.

questions.stem: Durability means what after a successful COMMIT, even if the server crashes moments later?
options: Committed effects survive crashes and can be recovered from durable storage
Committed data lives only in the buffer pool until the next checkpoint
Committed rows are immediately replicated to every geo region
Committed transactions can still be undone by any concurrent reader
questions.explanation: Durability: once COMMIT succeeds, effects persist across failures (typically via WAL/fsync).

questions.stem: OLTP versus OLAP — how should you contrast them in a systems interview?
options: OLTP is analytics warehouses; OLAP is high-QPS point updates
OLTP: many short transactional writes; OLAP: heavy analytical scans/aggregations
They are identical workloads with different marketing names
OLAP never uses indexes; OLTP never uses joins
questions.explanation: OLTP is transactional operational traffic; OLAP is analytical reporting over large scans.

questions.stem: What is a superkey in relational theory terms, at foundation interview level?
options: A key that must include every column of the relation
Only the single-column surrogate primary key chosen by the DBA
Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies a row, possibly with extras
A foreign key that points to a primary key in the same table
questions.explanation: A superkey uniquely identifies tuples; a candidate key is a minimal superkey.

questions.stem: Third normal form (3NF): which dependency pattern must you remove beyond 2NF?
options: Partial dependencies of non-keys on part of a composite key only
Any functional dependency involving a foreign key column
All multivalued dependencies regardless of keys
Transitive dependencies of non-key attributes on other non-key attributes
questions.explanation: 3NF eliminates transitive FDs: non-key → non-key chains that bypass the key.

questions.stem: What is the high-level idea of a write-ahead log (WAL) in recovery-oriented DBMS design?
options: Log the change durably before the corresponding data page is considered safe
Buffer pool pages are flushed before any log record is written
Delete the log after every successful SELECT to save disk space
Store only SELECT statements because writes never need recovery
questions.explanation: WAL: append redo/undo information to the log before relying on data-page durability.

questions.stem: Denormalization tradeoff: when might a team intentionally duplicate data across tables?
options: To always improve write throughput and reduce anomaly risk
To speed hot read paths at the cost of harder consistency on writes
To satisfy 3NF when a schema is already in BCNF
To eliminate the need for any primary keys forever
questions.explanation: Denormalization optimizes reads; writes must update multiple places and risk anomalies.

questions.stem: What does a clustered index conceptually determine about physical row storage?
options: Only which columns appear in the SELECT list of views
That no secondary indexes are allowed on the same table
The on-disk row order roughly follows the clustered key order
That all queries must use hash joins exclusively
questions.explanation: Clustered indexes organize table data by key order (engine-specific details vary).

questions.stem: Nonrepeatable read versus dirty read — which distinction should you give in an interview?
options: Both always mean reading uncommitted data from another transaction
Dirty reads are impossible in any isolation level by definition
Nonrepeatable reads only occur at SERIALIZABLE isolation
Dirty: uncommitted data; nonrepeatable: same row reread after another's commit changes it
questions.explanation: Dirty reads see uncommitted data; nonrepeatable reads see committed updates between reads.

questions.stem: At READ UNCOMMITTED, which anomaly is characteristically allowed that stronger levels prevent?
options: Dirty reads of another transaction's uncommitted writes
Only phantom inserts after the transaction ends
Lost updates that SERIALIZABLE always permits
Split-brain replica divergence across data centers
questions.explanation: READ UNCOMMITTED permits dirty reads; higher levels block seeing uncommitted data.

questions.stem: READ COMMITTED typically prevents dirty reads but still allows which classic anomaly?
options: Seeing another transaction's uncommitted INSERT forever
Nonrepeatable reads when a row changes between two reads
No anomalies of any kind by definition
Only write skew and never nonrepeatable reads
questions.explanation: READ COMMITTED stops dirty reads but still allows nonrepeatable reads and phantoms.

questions.stem: REPEATABLE READ: what does it generally protect against compared with READ COMMITTED?
options: All phantoms in every SQL engine without exception
Network partitions between primary and replica nodes
Nonrepeatable reads of already-read rows within the transaction
Schema changes from concurrent DDL in other sessions
questions.explanation: REPEATABLE READ keeps previously read row versions stable; phantoms may still appear (engine-dependent).

questions.stem: What is a phantom read in the ANSI isolation anomaly taxonomy used in interviews?
options: Rereading a known row ID and seeing an updated column value
Seeing uncommitted deletes that are later rolled back only
Reading a page that was never written to the buffer pool
A new row matching a prior predicate appears on a later query
questions.explanation: Phantoms: range/predicate results change because another transaction inserted/deleted matching rows.

questions.stem: SERIALIZABLE isolation aims for which correctness intuition relative to concurrent schedules?
options: Effects equivalent to some serial one-at-a-time order of transactions
Maximum throughput with zero locking under all workloads
Allowing dirty reads to reduce latch contention
Guaranteeing every query uses a covering index
questions.explanation: Serializable means the outcome matches some serial execution order of the transactions.

questions.stem: BCNF versus 3NF: when does BCNF demand a stronger decomposition than 3NF?
options: Whenever a relation has any foreign key at all
When a determinant of a functional dependency is not a candidate key
Only when the relation has no candidate keys
When all attributes are prime attributes already
questions.explanation: BCNF: every nontrivial FD's left side must be a superkey; 3NF allows some exceptions for primes.

questions.stem: Surrogate key versus natural key — what tradeoff do interviewers often expect?
options: Natural keys never change; surrogates always encode business meaning
Surrogates forbid indexes; natural keys require full table scans
Surrogates are stable opaque IDs; natural keys carry meaning but may change
Natural keys are illegal in 3NF; surrogates are mandatory
questions.explanation: Surrogates (e.g. UUID/serial) avoid coupling identity to mutable business attributes.

questions.stem: Covering index idea: why can a query avoid heap/table lookups after an index seek?
options: Because the optimizer disables all joins for that query
Because foreign keys automatically cover every SELECT list
Because covering indexes store the entire database in RAM only
Because all needed columns are present in the index leaf entries
questions.explanation: An index-only scan works when the index contains every column the query needs.

questions.stem: Checkpointing in a WAL-based system primarily helps with what operational concern?
options: Bounding recovery time by limiting how far back redo must replay
Eliminating the need for any redo on crash forever
Converting all B-tree indexes into hash indexes overnight
Making READ UNCOMMITTED identical to SERIALIZABLE
questions.explanation: Checkpoints flush dirty pages so crash recovery need not replay from the beginning of time.

questions.stem: Lost update anomaly: two transactions read a balance, each add money, both write. What went wrong?
options: The database refused both COMMITs due to durable WAL rules
One transaction's write silently overwrote the other's increment
Phantoms appeared because of a missing foreign key
The clustered index was converted to a heap mid-transaction
questions.explanation: Lost update: concurrent read-modify-write without proper locking/versioning drops one change.

questions.stem: MVCC high-level: how do readers often avoid blocking writers in modern engines?
options: By always taking exclusive locks on every SELECT
By disabling the write-ahead log during SELECT statements
By reading a snapshot version instead of waiting for writers' locks
By converting all tables to temporary tables per query
questions.explanation: MVCC serves older row versions to readers so writers can proceed without blocking reads.

questions.stem: When would you argue for denormalizing a frequently joined dimension into a fact table?
options: When write QPS is the only metric and reads never matter
When foreign keys are banned by company policy
When the schema must remain strictly in BCNF forever
When a hot analytical/OLTP read path pays too much for repeated joins
questions.explanation: Duplicate dimension attributes to cut join cost when reads dominate and staleness is managed.

questions.stem: Unique index versus unique constraint — what practical framing do interviewers expect?
options: A unique constraint is typically enforced via a unique index under the hood
They are unrelated; indexes never enforce uniqueness
Unique indexes forbid NULLs in every SQL dialect identically
Constraints live only in the application; indexes live only in RAM
questions.explanation: Engines usually implement uniqueness constraints with unique indexes.

questions.stem: Selectivity of a column: why does it matter when choosing which indexes to create?
options: Low-selectivity columns always make the best single-column indexes
High selectivity (many distinct values) often makes indexes more useful for filters
Selectivity only matters for hash indexes, never B-trees
The optimizer ignores selectivity and only counts table size
questions.explanation: Indexes help most when predicates discriminate; low-cardinality columns often filter poorly.

questions.stem: Two-phase locking (2PL) intuition: what must hold for the growing and shrinking phases?
options: Locks may be acquired after the first lock release at any time
All locks are released before any data page is read
Acquire locks in a growing phase; after first release, only shrink — no new locks
Shared locks are never upgraded and exclusive locks never release
questions.explanation: Strict/basic 2PL: no lock acquisition after unlocking begins, supporting serializable schedules.

questions.stem: Star schema for OLAP: what is the typical relationship between fact and dimension tables?
options: Facts must be in BCNF; dimensions must violate 1NF
One giant denormalized table with no foreign keys by definition
Only dimension tables store numeric measures; facts store labels
Many normalized dimensions join to a central fact table of measures
questions.explanation: Facts hold measures/FKs; dimensions hold descriptive attributes — classic warehouse modeling.

questions.stem: Composite index (a, b): which query predicate is most likely to use the index efficiently?
options: WHERE a = 1 AND b = 2 matching leftmost prefix
WHERE b = 5 with no predicate on a
WHERE c = 9 on an unrelated column only
ORDER BY b DESC without referencing a at all
questions.explanation: B-tree composites use the leftmost prefix; filtering only on trailing columns often cannot seek.

questions.stem: Idempotent retry of a committed money transfer: which DBMS design concern is central?
options: Making every SELECT use SERIALIZABLE by default
Detecting duplicate requests so a second COMMIT does not double-apply
Replacing WAL with synchronous full-table dumps
Denormalizing balances into every related table
questions.explanation: At-least-once delivery needs idempotency keys/unique constraints so retries are safe.

questions.stem: Buffer pool / page cache role: what does it optimize in a disk-backed DBMS?
options: Parsing SQL text into abstract syntax trees only
Enforcing foreign keys without storing any metadata
Caching frequently used pages in memory to reduce disk I/O
Compressing network packets between client drivers
questions.explanation: The buffer pool keeps hot pages in RAM so reads/writes avoid immediate disk hits.

questions.stem: Functional dependency X → Y means what in normalization discussions at interview depth?
options: Y can take any value independent of X in every legal relation instance
X must be a foreign key referencing Y's table
X and Y must both be candidate keys of the relation
For each X value, at most one Y value appears in any valid instance
questions.explanation: X → Y: X determines Y; equal X implies equal Y in every valid database state.

questions.stem: Write skew under SNAPSHOT isolation: two doctors both see one on-call, each goes off-duty. Why can both commit?
options: Each transaction's predicate check used a stale snapshot without mutual exclusion
Because dirty reads are required at SNAPSHOT isolation
SERIALIZABLE always allows write skew by definition
WAL replay reorders their COMMITs into a serial history
questions.explanation: Write skew: disjoint writes based on a shared predicate that neither locked/updated.

questions.stem: SSI (Serializable Snapshot Isolation) aims to fix which gap relative to plain SI?
options: It removes the need for durable logging entirely
It detects/aborts dangerous dependency cycles so SI behaves serializable
It forces every query to use hash indexes only
It makes CAP Consistency identical to ACID Atomicity
questions.explanation: SSI watches rw-dependencies and aborts transactions that would violate serializability.

questions.stem: Group commit / fsync batching: what latency-versus-throughput tradeoff are you describing?
options: Flushing each COMMIT with its own fsync maximizes throughput always
Skipping WAL entirely removes both latency and durability risk
Batching several COMMITs per durable flush improves throughput, may add latency
Group commit only applies to SELECT-only workloads
questions.explanation: Amortize expensive fsync across many transactions; wait a bit for a fuller batch.

questions.stem: Hot-row contention on a single counter row: which senior mitigation is most on-point?
options: Lower isolation to READ UNCOMMITTED for that update only
Replace the B-tree with a full table scan plan
Add more foreign keys pointing at the contested row
Shard counters, use queues, or avoid a single global hotspot row
questions.explanation: Serialize on one row kills scale; design around partitioning or asynchronous aggregation.

questions.stem: Logical decoding / CDC from WAL: what senior use case does this unlock?
options: Streaming row changes to search indexes, caches, or warehouses reliably
Replacing all secondary indexes with full table scans
Making phantom reads impossible without isolation changes
Eliminating the need for primary keys in OLTP schemas
questions.explanation: CDC reads the log stream to propagate changes without dual-writing from the app.

questions.stem: Online schema change without long exclusive locks — what technique is interview-relevant?
options: ALTER TABLE in one blocking transaction on multi-TB tables always
Shadow table/trigger or row-copy tools that swap with brief locks
Dropping all indexes first so DDL becomes instantaneous and safe
Running DDL only inside READ UNCOMMITTED sessions
questions.explanation: Tools copy/backfill to a new structure then cut over, avoiding prolonged table locks.

questions.stem: Quorum reads/writes in a replicated store: how does this relate to CAP Consistency?
options: Any quorum size always guarantees linearizability under all partitions
Quorums only improve Availability and never Consistency
Overlapping read/write quorums can provide strong consistency when reachable
Quorums replace the need for conflict resolution in multi-master forever
questions.explanation: R+W > N style quorums intersect so a read can see the latest successful write.

questions.stem: Index write amplification under heavy inserts: what senior observation should you make?
options: Every secondary index is free because WAL already records the row
Hash indexes never need maintenance on INSERT
More indexes always reduce insert latency linearly
Each secondary index adds extra random I/O and log/index maintenance cost
questions.explanation: Indexes speed reads but tax every write; over-indexing hurts ingest and storage.

questions.stem: Predicate locking versus next-key locking for phantoms: what is the shared goal?
options: Prevent new rows from appearing in a scanned predicate/range
Allow dirty reads while blocking nonrepeatable reads only
Force all queries onto hash joins without indexes
Disable MVCC versioning for the duration of SELECT
questions.explanation: Both approaches lock ranges/predicates so concurrent inserts cannot create phantoms.

questions.stem: You must pick isolation for a banking ledger that forbids write skew and phantoms. Best default stance?
options: READ UNCOMMITTED with application-level hope
SERIALIZABLE or carefully proven equivalent patterns (SSI/strict 2PL)
READ COMMITTED because dirty reads never matter for money
No transactions — single-statement auto-commit only
questions.explanation: Financial correctness needs serializable semantics or rigorously equivalent controls.
